title: Branches: The RISC Way
lesson:
On the 6502 you compared with CPX and branched on a flag. The PDP-8 used ISZ. RISC simplifies the whole picture. MIPS has no condition codes. Branches read the registers directly: 'bne $a, $b, label' jumps to label if $a does not equal $b. That's it. Every branch in MIPS is a register-vs-register comparison plus a target.

This is the canonical countdown idiom: load a counter, do work, addi the counter by -1, and bne back to the top while the counter is not zero. You'll see exactly this loop in every RISC architecture you ever touch, including the LC-3 you'll meet next and the ARM chip in your phone.
prompt: Sum 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 (= 15) with a counted loop. Use $t0 as the counter (starting at 5), $t1 as the running sum. Each pass: add $t1 += $t0, addi $t0 -= 1, bne back while $t0 != $zero. Print the result with syscall 1.
starterCode:
.text
        li   $t0, 5            # counter
        li   $t1, 0            # sum
loop:
        add  $t1, $t1, $t0
        addi $t0, $t0, -1
        bne  $t0, $zero, loop
        move $a0, $t1          # print the sum
        li   $v0, 1
        syscall
        li   $v0, 10
        syscall
hints: bne $t0, $zero, loop branches back as long as $t0 is non-zero.
After the loop, copy $t1 into $a0 and print with syscall 1.
reference:
.text
li $t0, 5
li $t1, 0
loop:
add $t1, $t1, $t0
addi $t0, $t0, -1
bne $t0, $zero, loop
move $a0, $t1
li $v0, 1
syscall
li $v0, 10
syscall
